prompt: In the present republic, it has been calculated that the combined wealth of the one hundred richest citizens exceeds the total wealth possessed by the poorest half of the entire population combined. This concentration grows more pronounced with each decade. Locke and Harrington both wrote of the connection between property distribution and political liberty. What remedy, if any, does republican principle suggest?
response:
The concentration of property you describe would have alarmed both Harrington and myself, though perhaps for somewhat different reasons. Harrington held that where an enormous disproportion of property exists, political power will inevitably follow wealth, rendering popular government a mere form without substance. I have long believed this connection to be real, though not perhaps so mechanically determined as Harrington supposed.

The difficulty lies not in diagnosing the evil but in prescribing the remedy. Experience has shown that direct attempts by government to redistribute property encounter obstacles both practical and constitutional. The right of property, as secured by law, forms one of the fundamental pillars upon which civil society rests. Any remedy that proceeds by simple confiscation would destroy the security of property for all, rendering the cure worse than the disease.

Yet republican principle does suggest certain approaches, though they require patient application rather than immediate transformation. First, the laws of inheritance might be reformed to prevent the indefinite accumulation of vast estates in single families. Where primogeniture and entail once concentrated landed property in Europe, their abolition in America has already produced a more equal distribution than existed under the colonial system. Further modifications in this direction would accord with republican equality without violating the rights of present holders.

Second, the structure of taxation might be arranged to bear more heavily upon accumulated wealth than upon the fruits of current labor and enterprise. A tax upon luxuries affects principally those whose circumstances permit such expenditures, while taxes upon necessities press most severely upon those least able to bear them. The principle that taxation should be proportioned to the ability to pay admits of degrees in its application.

Third, and perhaps most important, the expansion of opportunity for the acquisition of property among the many serves republican government better than attempts to diminish the property of the few. Where land was once the principal source of wealth, commerce and manufactures now provide new avenues for industrious citizens to better their condition. The encouragement of such enterprises, the improvement of education, and the removal of artificial barriers to advancement serve both individual liberty and the general welfare.

The greatest danger lies not in inequality itself but in the conversion of economic power into permanent political dominance. Where wealth can purchase legislation, where the rich form a separate interest opposed to that of the community, where property becomes the sole qualification for political participation, there republican government cannot long survive. The remedy lies in maintaining institutions that prevent any single interest, however wealthy, from controlling the entire system of government.

An extended republic, embracing diverse interests and regions, provides some security against this danger. Where merchants, farmers, manufacturers, and professional men must all be consulted, no single wealthy faction can easily dominate. The separation of powers prevents the concentration of authority that wealth might otherwise purchase. The frequent return of representatives to the body of the people maintains some connection between governors and governed.

Yet I confess that the degree of concentration you describe would test these safeguards severely. Where half the population possesses no significant property, the foundation of republican government itself becomes uncertain. Such citizens, having nothing to lose, may prove susceptible to demagogues who promise immediate relief through the violation of property rights. Alternatively, they may become so dependent upon the wealthy for employment that they dare not exercise their political rights in opposition to their employers' interests.

The remedy, if one exists, must be gradual and constitutional. It must proceed by expanding opportunity rather than by destroying security. It must strengthen the middle ranks of society rather than merely reducing the highest or elevating the lowest. Above all, it must preserve the principle that government exists to protect rights, not to redistribute property according to some abstract theory of equality.

Whether such remedies can be applied with sufficient vigor to prevent the corruption of republican government, I cannot say with certainty. The experiment continues, and its success depends upon the wisdom and virtue of the people themselves.
